summary: Gruber argues that Microsoft's dominance in corporate computing is sustained not by product quality but by the self-interest of IT departments, who choose complex, high-maintenance systems to justify their own existence. He begins by explaining how most people buy computers based on recommendations from tech-savvy friends, then extends this dynamic to corporate IT, where the people choosing technology benefit from its unreliability. Using a vivid plumbing analogy, he argues that computing should be held to the same reliability standards as other infrastructure. He concludes that if companies demanded high reliability and low maintenance, alternatives to Microsoft — particularly Macs — would become far more attractive.
key_points: Microsoft achieved monopoly not by winning over the general population directly, but by winning over the nerds who make purchase recommendations for everyone else
Outlook/Exchange is the worst email system ever made by security metrics, yet the most popular — because it serves IT departments' interests, not users'
Corporate IT staffs have a perverse incentive to deploy high-maintenance technology, since their budgets and influence grow with headcount
Computing infrastructure should be held to the same reliability standards as plumbing, but society has been convinced to accept lower expectations for computers
Macs are more common in publishing precisely because those industries cannot tolerate downtime — reliability is a feature that undermines IT empire-building
money_quotes: Microsoft has always understood this. They've achieved an overwhelming monopoly not by targeting an overwhelming majority of the general population, but rather by targeting an overwhelming majority of people who are 'into computers'.
Fear of blowing $1000 or $2000 on a 'bad' computer motivates them to buy whatever is recommended by their closest nerd.
It is thus in the interests of corporate IT staff to deploy technology that requires a large IT staff for maintenance.

The corporate IT field is in large parts comprised of men who are not smart enough to program, but yet wanted a career in computers. They are the fifth wheel of corporate America — serving no practical purpose but their own employment.
The only difference between 'most people' and 'most corporate IT professionals' is that the IT guys aren't intimidated.
So am I claiming that Apple's market share suffers because its products are too reliable and require too little maintenance? Well, yes.
If you insist upon high reliability and low maintenance, solutions other than 'all-Microsoft, all-the-time' start to look very good indeed.
key_insight: Microsoft's dominance is sustained by a perverse incentive structure where the people who choose corporate technology benefit professionally from its complexity and unreliability.
